# Questions in Livestream 69

Live Stream #69: They Might Be Giants.

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp. The links use HTML anchors so GitHub can render them with `target="_blank"`.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gRDrvTEmitw?t=600" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">10:00</a> | Did Abraham likely use images in worship, and are there ancient Near Eastern examples of worship without images? | He said Abraham probably did use images, since that was normal for the period, while truly imageless worship does appear only sparsely, with a few unusual Egyptian examples like offerings before empty shrines or thrones. | He first notes rare Egyptian examples of offerings before empty shrines or thrones, including an unusual empty throne in a divine procession, but stresses that these are not common. For Abraham and the patriarchs, he thinks image use is probable because pre-Sinai religion differed from later Israelite practice, and he points to household idols, fertility imagery, ancestor images, and teraphim as evidence that such practices persisted among patriarchal and later Israelite settings. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gRDrvTEmitw?t=859" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">14:19</a> | What is the third heaven like metaphysically, and are space and time there only conceptual? | He said heaven still seems to involve causality, sequence, and a kind of space, but not under the same framework as ordinary physical space-time. | He defines the third heaven as the spiritual realm associated with God, angels, the heavenly host, and the souls of the deceased. Even if space and time are conceptual there, he says that does not make them unreal: worship before the throne in Revelation implies sequence, action, causality, and some kind of space, though under rules different from ordinary physical space-time. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gRDrvTEmitw?t=1034" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">17:14</a> | Do Judas Iscariot and Cain play central roles in real modern vampire-cult mythology? | He said Judas does not, but Cain sometimes does, especially through interpretations of the mark of Cain as a vampiric sign in some modern occult or vampire-religion circles. | He distinguishes contemporary vampire religions from popular vampire media: Judas Iscariot is not central, while Cain appears in some groups because the mark of Cain is interpreted as a sign of vampiric identity or origin. He names Temple of the Vampire literature as one place this appears, but qualifies that it is not universal and that Lilith is often a more popular figure in vampire-religion mythology. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gRDrvTEmitw?t=1361" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">22:41</a> | Is Seth in Genesis supposed to be Set, the Hyksos god? | No. He said the names are not really related, Set was an older Egyptian storm god later equated with Baal, and Genesis's Seth is Abel's replacement in the good line. | He rejects the identification on linguistic and mythological grounds. The Hebrew name Seth and Egyptian Set are not really etymologically alike, Set was not originally a Hyksos god but an ancient Egyptian storm god of the Delta later equated with Baal, and Genesis presents Seth as Abel's replacement rather than as a jealous or murderous mythic figure. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gRDrvTEmitw?t=1631" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">27:11</a> | Is there proof in Genesis that the serpent is Satan? | Not in Genesis by itself. He said the identification comes from later biblical passages, especially Revelation 12:9, with Ezekiel 28 sometimes discussed alongside that theme. | He says Genesis alone does not explicitly prove the serpent is Satan, but later biblical texts identify the serpent with Satan, with Revelation supplying the clearest link. He also adds a caveat that "Satan" can function as a title meaning accuser, so the Satan of Revelation is the serpent of the garden even if that does not settle every possible use of the title elsewhere, such as in Job. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gRDrvTEmitw?t=1881" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">31:21</a> | Were the Psalms always divided into five books, and why did Jesus give Mary to John rather than James? | He said the five-book Psalms division looks Masoretic, while earlier psalm collections were more fluid; he also said Jesus entrusted Mary to John because James did not yet believe and John had remained faithful at the cross. | On the Psalms, he says the five-book division appears to be Masoretic, while Dead Sea Scrolls evidence and the Septuagint show more fluid psalm collections, arrangements, and even Psalm 151. On Mary, he says Jesus entrusted her to John because James and Jesus's brothers did not yet believe, while John had stayed at the cross and had proven trustworthy enough to care for her. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gRDrvTEmitw?t=2279" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">37:59</a> | Do fringe alternative theories like Graham Hancock's help widen archaeology's scope? | No. He said such theories add confusion rather than useful scholarship, and he grouped them with other fringe or charlatan claims that ignore contrary evidence. | He answers sharply that fringe theories do not help archaeology or biblical scholarship because they add confusion, doubt, and sensational claims rather than evidence. He groups Hancock with figures such as David Rohl and Ron Wyatt, saying these approaches often profit from lay confusion while ignoring contrary evidence rather than advancing understanding. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gRDrvTEmitw?t=2467" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">41:07</a> | If Sheol is not a place of punishment, why is the rich man suffering in Luke 16? | He distinguished torment from final punishment and treated the passage as describing agony in Hades rather than the last judgment itself. | Reading Luke 16, he emphasizes that the text says the rich man is in torment or agony, not necessarily in final punishment. He argues those categories should be kept distinct: a person can suffer torment as the consequence of rebellion and exposure to holiness without that being the final judgment, so Hades can be a place of torment even if it is not the ultimate place of punishment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gRDrvTEmitw?t=2760" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">46:00</a> | Does Proverbs 8 support the Trinity, and is Wisdom there Jesus despite being personified as feminine? | He said no. In his reading, Proverbs 8 personifies wisdom as an idealized woman rather than presenting a literal divine person of the Trinity. | He identifies the speaker in Proverbs 8 as personified Wisdom and says the passage is a literary device, not a literal divine person or an allusion to Christ. He uses the question to stress that biblical texts employ metaphor, poetry, personification, and other literary forms, so Wisdom's feminine depiction supports reading the passage as personification rather than as a Trinitarian proof text. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gRDrvTEmitw?t=3041" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">50:41</a> | Why are Yeshua and Satan both called the morning star? | He said the morning star is a role-symbol tied to Venus and the dawn; Satan lost that role through rebellion, while Christ fulfills it as the herald of the coming new age. | He explains the morning star as Venus, the brightest heavenly body before dawn, making it a potent ancient symbol for the arrival of a new day. Applied to Christ, it marks the beginning and fullness of the new age; applied to Satan in Isaiah 14, it points to a lost role or status as a once-exalted light-bringer, with that role transferred to one who fulfills it for God's glory. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gRDrvTEmitw?t=3490" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">58:10</a> | Did Yeshua meet Peter before the miracle of the fish in John 1:35-42? | He leaned yes from John's sequence, but said he had not studied closely enough to be fully certain how it aligns with the fishing-miracle account. | After reading John 1, he says the passage appears to show Andrew bringing Simon Peter to Jesus before the fishing miracle, but he cautions that Gospel ordering is not always strictly chronological. Because biblical narratives can be arranged didactically rather than sequentially, he leaves open whether John's account precedes or dovetails with the fishing-miracle tradition. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gRDrvTEmitw?t=3814" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:03:34</a> | Is the rapture biblical? | He said the idea is biblical in the broad sense that it comes from biblical texts, but he rejected the standard pre-tribulational Left Behind style system as resting on too many assumptions. | He distinguishes whether an idea comes from biblical texts from whether a specific interpretation is correct. He says the common pre-tribulational "Left Behind" model depends on assumptions he is uncomfortable with, and he suggests that some passages about one being taken may describe the removal and judgment of unbelievers rather than a desirable removal of believers. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gRDrvTEmitw?t=4226" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:10:26</a> | Does salvation make Abraham's bosom obsolete, and can people still go there when they die? | He said Abraham's bosom no longer serves a purpose after Christ's redemptive work, so believers now go directly to heaven rather than to that intermediate compartment. | He frames Abraham's bosom as an answer to the old theological problem of faithful dead people awaiting forgiveness before they could enter God's presence. Once Christ provides forgiveness of sins, that compartment in Sheol or Hades has no remaining function, so he says believers no longer go there and would now go directly to heaven. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gRDrvTEmitw?t=4377" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:12:57</a> | Why did Egypt not have good food security, and what about nobles, pharaohs, and other ancient Near Eastern cultures? | He said Egypt's food security depended heavily on Nile inundation levels driven by East African rains; even nobles could face ration limits, while Mesopotamia had more consistent rainfall and easier food planning. | He says Egypt's agricultural strength was also its weakness because food production depended on Nile inundation, which in turn depended on rainfall in East Africa. Low floods meant canals were not replenished, fields were not fertilized by silt, and people starved; even nobles and princes show evidence of rationing and malnutrition, while Mesopotamia could plan food more consistently because its rainfall was more stable. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gRDrvTEmitw?t=4809" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:20:09</a> | What pattern should be noticed across the listed passages about Ham, Canaan, Abraham, Isaac, and David? | He said he could not clearly see the intended pattern in the live setting, though he recognized a weak possible link involving adultery and exposing another man's nakedness. | He works through some of the cited passages live but says the list is too much to process in the moment and that he may simply be missing the intended pattern after a long day. The one possible connection he acknowledges is that adultery can expose another man's nakedness, which may weakly connect some wife-sister and David/Bathsheba material with the Ham/Canaan theme, but he does not endorse a firm reading. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gRDrvTEmitw?t=5138" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:25:38</a> | Are the twelve gems in Revelation an ancient Near Eastern motif? | He connected them chiefly to the twelve stones on the high priest's breastplate and the twelve tribes of Israel, saying the specific stones differ but the symbolic number matters. | He says Revelation's twelve gems chiefly recall the twelve stones on the high priest's breastplate, where each stone represented one tribe of Israel. The exact stone identifications differ between Exodus and Revelation, but in his reading the symbolic point is the number twelve and the tribal symbolism, especially in connection with the New Jerusalem and God's people. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gRDrvTEmitw?t=5345" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:29:05</a> | What does Proverbs 3:5-6 really mean? | He said it does not forbid understanding or reason; it warns against relying on one's own understanding as ultimate instead of trusting God. | He reads "do not lean on your own understanding" as a warning against making one's own judgment the sole support, not as a command to stop thinking or learning. He argues that using teachers, commentaries, scholarship, and the wider wisdom God provides is consistent with trusting God, while isolated self-reliance is exactly what the proverb warns against. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gRDrvTEmitw?t=5817" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:36:57</a> | Is feet washing a euphemism for sex in Genesis 19:2? | No. He said washing feet is a hospitality practice, though he separately noted that raising a garment above the ankle could carry sexual overtones in some contexts. | He says Lot's offer to wash the visitors' feet is ordinary Middle Eastern hospitality, especially for sandal-wearing travelers with dusty feet, and compares it to Jesus washing the disciples' feet. The caveat is that raising a garment above the ankle could have sexual overtones in some ancient and modern Middle Eastern contexts, but that is separate from foot washing itself. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gRDrvTEmitw?t=6051" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:40:51</a> | Are the signs mentioned by Josephus, Tacitus, and Cassius Dio around AD 60-70 embellishments or historical observations? | He said he had not studied those particular reports closely enough, but he tends to treat recent-event signs as possibly observational while allowing that some could be literary embellishment. | He says he has not read those specific reports closely enough to judge them, though he has identified some ancient omen reports elsewhere, such as chariots in the sky possibly reflecting a locust swarm. His tentative instinct is that signs reported close to the author's own time may be observational, but he explicitly leaves room for literary embellishment until he can read the sources directly. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gRDrvTEmitw?t=6312" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:45:12</a> | Could the Cave of the Patriarchs or the traditional tomb of Aaron be evidence for Abraham or Aaron? | He dismissed Aaron's tomb tradition as highly doubtful and treated Abraham's cave only as weak circumstantial evidence supported by tradition and broadly fitting archaeology. | He says Aaron's traditional tomb is highly suspect and not useful as evidence. For the Cave of Machpelah, he notes long-standing tradition and archaeological evidence of early and middle Bronze Age Canaanite occupation that fits Abraham's period, but he rates this only as weak circumstantial evidence that moves probability slightly rather than proving the identification. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gRDrvTEmitw?t=6528" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:48:48</a> | If kosher laws no longer bind Christians, how should Isaiah 66:15-17 be understood when it condemns eating swine and mice? | He read the passage as judgment on idolatrous rebellion rather than as proof that new-covenant believers remain under kosher law. | He reads Isaiah 66 as prophetic and symbolic rather than as a simple end-times reaffirmation of kosher law. The combination of swine, detestable things, and mice functions for him as a literary signal of broader abominable practices, such as idolatry and rebellion, with the absurdity of eating mice forcing the reader to look beyond diet to a higher principle. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gRDrvTEmitw?t=7149" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:59:09</a> | Which other scholars date the Pentateuch early and in Egypt because of cultural similarities, and why do mainstream scholars date it late? | He named Benjamin Noonan, Joshua Berman, Alan Millard, James Hoffmeier, and Chris McKinny, then said many mainstream scholars remain shaped by the documentary hypothesis and often ignore or explain away Egyptian details. | He names Benjamin Noonan, Joshua Berman, Alan Millard, James Hoffmeier, and Chris McKinny as scholars broadly sympathetic to early dating or strong Egyptian influence in the Pentateuch. He says many mainstream scholars, especially in North America, remain trained in documentary-hypothesis frameworks, so they often do not engage Egyptian details, ignore them, or explain them away with a low evidentiary threshold. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gRDrvTEmitw?t=7389" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:03:09</a> | What are his thoughts on Deuteronomy 23:1-3 as ritual cleanliness rather than moral standards? | He agreed that the passage concerns ritual purity and access to the assembly or sacred space, not moral guilt for bodily condition, birth status, or ethnicity. | He says the "assembly of the Lord" concerns access to the temple or tabernacle precincts, where ritual cleanness and covenantal normalcy governed entry into sacred space. Conditions such as bodily defect, illegitimate birth, or non-Israelite status are not moral guilt; they mark categories outside the ritual standards of Israel's sacred space until later temple arrangements such as the court of the Gentiles. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gRDrvTEmitw?t=7769" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:09:29</a> | What does he think of identifying Babel with Eridu in Iraq? | He was skeptical and said the Eridu proposal depends on exceptionally thin and fragmentary evidence. | He criticizes the Eridu/Babel proposal as an overasserted hypothesis lacking the extraordinary evidence it would need. Because the relevant early Mesopotamian inscriptional material is fragmentary and because he doubts the proponent has the necessary Akkadian or Sumerian tools, he treats the claim as far beyond what the evidence can currently support. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gRDrvTEmitw?t=8020" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:13:40</a> | Can the JEP or documentary hypothesis be applied to the Septuagint or other ancient Near Eastern literature? | He said scholars have tried applying it to the Septuagint with mixed results, and the outcome depends heavily on which criteria, such as doublets or changes in person, are used. | He says scholars have tried applying documentary-hypothesis methods to the Septuagint and that the results are mixed. The outcome depends on the criteria used: doublets may produce one kind of result, while changes in person or comparison with the Masoretic Text and Samaritan Pentateuch can produce different source divisions, raising questions about the method. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gRDrvTEmitw?t=8306" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:18:26</a> | Why do some scholars reject the idea that the Hyksos were ancestors of the early Israelites? | He said Hyksos rulers and the later Israelites should not simply be equated: both relate to Semitic populations at Avaris, but they were not the same group and `Israelites` is itself anachronistic before the Exodus. | He says "Israelites" is anachronistic before the Exodus; before Sinai they are the sons of Jacob among Amorite or Asiatic Semites. The Hyksos were foreign rulers of Avaris, not the whole Semitic population, and when those rulers fled they left many Semitic groups behind, so the later Israelites may share cultural affinity with populations at Avaris without descending from the Hyksos rulers themselves. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gRDrvTEmitw?t=8640" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:24:00</a> | What does he think of Angela Mazzifra's article on a sixteenth-century BC destruction layer at Hazor? | He said he would need to read the article and site report before judging it; as described, the layer would be Middle Bronze and too early for most Exodus chronologies. | He says he would need to read both the article and the site report before forming a real judgment. Based only on the description, a sixteenth-century BC destruction layer would be Middle Bronze and too early for both most early-date and late-date Exodus chronologies, so he does not see its immediate importance for biblical-history arguments without more detail. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gRDrvTEmitw?t=8810" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:26:50</a> | Which Egyptian god is best, and why is it Thoth? | He agreed with Thoth, citing roles tied to scribes, writing, magic, the moon, medicine, a broad cult, and the unusual involvement of commoners in Thoth's service. | He praises Thoth as god of scribes, writing, magic, the moon, and medicine, and says Egyptian writing's mnemonic system makes that association especially fitting for a scholar. He also notes that Thoth's cult was unusual because commoners could serve him, unlike most Egyptian gods served by kings, and that his major center at Hermopolis Magna had a long and interesting history. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gRDrvTEmitw?t=9213" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:33:33</a> | What is the estimated timeline for his book about the ten plagues? | He said it is still a long way off because writing it would require substantial uninterrupted time or sabbatical support, though he still wants to produce it. | He says the ten plagues book is a long way off because serious writing would require sustained time that he does not currently have while balancing a full-time job and YouTube work. He still wants it to be a richly illustrated bridge between lay and academic writing, similar in style to his Ark book, but says it likely depends on funding or sabbatical support. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gRDrvTEmitw?t=9542" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:39:02</a> | Was Sheshonq a political opportunist exploiting Israel's division by attacking Jeroboam's lands? | Yes. He said Sheshonq was opportunistic, used division and intrigue, and attacked both kingdoms when they were divided in order to weaken and pillage the Levant. | He says Sheshonq was very much an opportunist who liked division and intrigue, including sheltering Jeroboam earlier as a way to pressure Solomon. Once Israel and Judah were divided, Sheshonq attacked both kingdoms at a vulnerable moment to weaken the Levant and pillage it, so the questioner's political reading is essentially right. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gRDrvTEmitw?t=9693" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:41:33</a> | From which Mesopotamian population was Abram descended? | He said Abram was from the Amorites of northern Mesopotamia around Urkesh, likely associated with the king of Mari rather than with southern Ur. | He identifies Abram with Amorites from northern Mesopotamia, especially the region around Urkesh, rather than with Leonard Woolley's southern Ur. He says that region changed hands, but Abram's family was probably most associated with Mari because Terah was allied with the king of Mari before the family moved toward Syria and the Levant. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gRDrvTEmitw?t=9848" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:44:08</a> | Could the Merneptah Stele's reference to Israel's seed be a pun on Jezreel, meaning God will sow? | He said it is possible and not beyond Egyptian poetic practice, though he would need to look into the meaning and proposal further before judging it firmly. | He says the proposed Jezreel pun is possible because Egyptian poets were capable of double meanings, amphiboly, homonyms, and other literary devices. He does not claim it is established, noting that he had not realized the proposed meaning of Jezreel and would need to investigate further before forming a firm opinion. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gRDrvTEmitw?t=10144" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:49:04</a> | How could Jesus's physical body enter heaven if heaven is a spiritual realm? | He said Scripture already has bodily ascension precedents such as Enoch and Elijah, and heaven seems to have causality, space, time, and rules that make bodily entrance not obviously incompatible. | He points to Enoch and Elijah as earlier biblical examples of bodily ascent, then says we do not fully understand the mechanics of either matter or the heavenly realm. Because heaven appears to have causality, some notion of space and time, and its own rules or parameters, a physical body entering that realm is mysterious but not obviously incompatible. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gRDrvTEmitw?t=10558" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:55:58</a> | Did Egyptians really use cheetahs as hunting animals? | He said they clearly kept exotic animals and used trained dogs and cats for hunting, but he was unsure whether cheetahs were actually trained for hunting and would need to check the evidence. | He says Egyptians certainly kept exotic animals in zoos or as elite display animals, including cheetahs, leopards, baboons, elephants, giraffes, and sometimes rhinos. For hunting, he is confident about trained dogs and domestic cats, but uncertain about cheetahs; they might have been used in contexts like lion hunts, yet he would need to check reliefs and evidence before saying so. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gRDrvTEmitw?t=10961" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:02:41</a> | Why are there no hieratic or demotic Unicode fonts? | He said demotic relies on whole-word ligatures rather than simple letters, while hieratic varies by period and sign form, making both font design and character encoding difficult. | He explains that demotic is not written as simple letters but as ligatures, often whole-word ligatures, which makes ordinary character encoding and font design extremely hard. Hieratic has a different problem: it changes across Egyptian periods, includes variant signs and period-specific forms, and would likely need whole families of fonts and encodings, while even hieroglyphic Unicode support remains incomplete for extended signs. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/gRDrvTEmitw?t=11547" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:12:27</a> | Is it worth studying Paleo-Hebrew or early alphabetic inscriptions? | Yes. He strongly encouraged it, arguing that Northwest Semitic epigraphy is valuable background for biblical study and badly needs more qualified specialists. | He reframes the subject as Paleo-Canaanite or early alphabetic inscriptions and says studying West Semitic epigraphy is absolutely worthwhile for biblical backgrounds. It is difficult because it requires strong Semitic languages and epigraphy, but he argues the field needs more knowledgeable specialists because a lack of expertise leaves room for unreliable claims. |
